item.title: Everest MiCA CASP — 166 replies — 664.0/hr
item.content:encoded:
Everest MiCA CASP

Open thread • Replies: 166 • Updated: 2026-07-21 12:33 UTC

OP

I want to personally thank every Everest ID hater in here, talking to you Link baggies. They secured MiCA CASP last week.

A single MFSA license is valid across 27 EU states, reaching ~450M people. Of the ~283 CASPs on the ESMA register, nearly all are exchanges or brokers. Everest is one of the rare licensed infrastructure providers: identity and payment rails, not another venue.

Enjoy it furries

Too many baggies were created and many are still holding on, including whales. I see that across a lot of the stuff I’m in. So if we ever did have a run up I feel like the sell pressure would be worse than before because everyone that’s still holding will want to exit with whatever profits they can get. All the shitcoins with little liquidity are going to get nuked because of how pvp it will get. I’m a baggie who round tripped a mid-upper 6 figure bag across a bunch of projects and I can tell you right now that my standards of what I consider to be “making it” is far less than before. idgaf about making 7 figures anymore. 62497947
>>62497771
they pulled the liquidity but they didn't run so that's a good sign on it not being a scam, they did it by doing an airdrop. They say they are still solvent but that just means that in conjunction with pulling the liquidity shows that avf is on probably running on fumes and doesn't have much operational budget left, Unless it can see some success. That said I think the arcade beta is actually really good.

Stixil's problem is that he care's too much about the chart and not enough about the business and makes some decisions to help the chart, that end up hurting the business.

I'm cautiously optimistic. Optimistic since I do think the arcade is a good idea. but i'm also cautious because of having to reassure solvency just means its something that stixil thinks that people would be worried about, which means its something that he might be worried about, which means we're running out of runway.

62497950
Stixil is playing games with my money now. either make the price go up or give me my refund. nothing you promised me happened, where is my cent per avi? you promised me my cent and now it’s time to pay up. who is going to buy my coins now? how about you give all of your holders USDC at value of their AVI holdings and be done with this little circus? it’s time to pay up your time has come to pay us

Signaling they are out of funds (red flag) then requesting users that already got rug pulled via liquidity grab to then put in more money for liquidity (another red flag). Perhaps all i see is red flags and there are some green flags but its not looking good

I've already accepted that i burned my money

62501920
>>62501458

Let’s be honest here. Stixil isn’t coming up with, let alone building, a solution to all of cryptos problems. Stratus will be the final chapter to this project. I can’t see anyone sticking around after that, holders and the team. It just is t worth the time or money

>>62481067

Dead shitcoin

62504074
>>62504020
>>62504035
Checking my ID get. Well I was there before GME went 50x. I’ve held crypto parabolas. PLTR saved my port in 2024-25 when it became a 30 bagger. So I started to ask myself, if I’ve held through all these huge moves, then wtf haven’t I made it yet? And I realized that there were a couple of problems. 1) I didn’t have enough initial exposure. You need to go in big to get paid out big. So at least $10-25k because $25k turning into a tenbagger is $250k. That’s enough to buy a house, whether or not you actually want one. 2) selling too soon, or just because you are up, or because you’re afraid it will fall. I should have sold 10% at a time every time I felt like selling. Keeps going up from there? Good, I still have more. Starts to fall? Good, at least I sold some at the top. I’ve learned you’re never going to sell the top, so selling all of it at once just makes sure you make a mistake.
Anyways MU looked like the ticket in January and it played out. Now, I’m not so sure. Things are very shaky. Maybe it is time for the hyperfaggers to shine, maybe it isn’t. But it’s not clear enough for me to go all in one of them. Good luck to you.
But if it actually starts falling, cut your loses and get out. You don’t want to become a baggie.

62504153
I think that's great. I bought like close to 50 shares at that about same price and we're doing good. Just don't get greedy when it goes to like 450 500 start selling off chunks taking profits moving them into safer broad index ETFs. But all the analysts seem to agree for what it's worth this things projections in the high 400 range to low 600 range.

62504181
>>62502286
Your feelings are right. Somehow you've gicen custody of your financial future to 1 (one) company. This is what economists and psychologists call "retarded". One reason I still visit /biz/ is because it is a comedic hurtbox of people who keep trapping their entire net worth in single stocks. This board attracts retards like shit attracts flies. Blind to risk at the time of purchase. Sunk cost effect kicks in at the drawdowns, and the human 'doubles down', because he 'is right'. Many such cases ... well, at least you're in Microshart. Could be worse. Others here choose to let their future be decided by single individuals like vitalik and sirgay. It's like a coinflip but worse. Diversification is not a meme. Risk aversion is not a meme. Your 'best company' can go -40% in a day because of circumstance. -30% in a day has happened to me many times. item.title: Why is there so much negativity surrounding the UK? — 126 replies — 52.5/hr
item.content:encoded:
Why is there so much negativity surrounding the UK?

Open thread • Replies: 126 • Updated: 2026-07-21 10:21 UTC

OP

Every time I see the UK mentioned here, it is always in a negative light.

It is always
>In the USA, wages are better!
>In Europe, houses are cheaper!
>The FTSE is underperforming!
>My taxes are too high!

There is never any mention of the fantastic benefits which come with life in the UK. The fantastic healthcare for minimal cost, the great education system available to everybody, the dynamic modern economy with many bustling sectors, and everything else.

Taxes are higher than the USA but they are lower than most of Europe and we still enjoy fantastic public services whereas in America there are no public services or safety nets.

Not to mention the things you cannot measure. Being able to walk the streets in safety without fear of gun violence and gangs. Enjoying the fantastic beautiful nature and gardens. Living in the historical majesty and balancing that with modern cosmopolitan qualities.

The UK might not be the best place to become rich but nobody is poor here and it is very easy to enjoy life if you have goals beyond the number in your bank account.

62502616
>>62481986
>So just right wing racebaiting?
No he has a point, those immigrants has to be paid for by someone, and while a few are fine- there come a breaking point where social services cannot sustain a large number of them while also being functional, and that number is inversely correlated to how many social services the native population receives. A well-cared for native population with migrants dumped on them will find themselves incapable of fielding a diverse array of social services.

Those social services are the glue that keep nations from becoming third world tier, among other factors. A nation needs to be able to have modern public infrastructure, education, health care, housing, transportation, utilities, welfare, unemployment. This is why every business, despite their bluster, doesn't actually move their corporate HQs and operations to nations where labor is extremely cheap like India or Africa: They still need a nation that is capable of filling in the gaps these corporations won't do themselves like have safe roads and reliable energy.

The folly of using mass migration to fix population pyramid woes is that somebody has to pay for someone who is fresh off the boat, and it could realistically never be the first generation or even statistically the second generation migrant. It's great for companies who want to bust all their unions, though.

Unfortunately, there is no such thing as a leadership that has ever chosen to 'go back'. Those villas and quiet farms are quiet for a reason: Everyone has to move someplace else to work.

If the BEM strait is closed oil will flow north through Suez to London where Brent is priced, no disruption there.

All the BEM closure will do is make it more expensive for Asia to buy oil but there's no investable Asian oil futures market. At best ship fuel demand goes up because more is required to go to Asia. That's a refined product though.

Basically what's going to have to happen is products are going to have to moon so much that China is greedy enough to start re-importing oil to make money off the products.

But what will happen ultimately (let's say by 2030) is more oil will be exported directly from The Americas to Asia and there will be more bypass pipeline capacity built out to transport the oil overland if necessary.

American (North and South) oil producers and integrated refiners look good long term.
